title: How To Deal With Doubts and Doubters
excerpt: In How to Deal With Doubts and Doubters (1907, Revised 2025), Henry Clay Trumbull opens the door to real, heart-level conversations with troubled souls—men...
description:
When doubt feels louder than truth, where do you turn? In How to Deal With Doubts and Doubters (1907, Revised 2025), Henry Clay Trumbull opens the door to real, heart-level conversations with troubled souls—men and women who feared they had gone too far, committed the “unpardonable sin,” lacked true faith, or could not feel what they thought a real Christian should feel. These are not theories, but actual encounters drawn from decades of ministry as a chaplain, pastor, and personal worker. Through vivid stories and clear, Christ-centered counsel, Trumbull shows:
  • How to help those who focus on what they don’t believe learn to rest in what they do believe
  • Why endlessly examining your feelings destroys assurance—and where to look instead
  • How to speak to people who think they must “feel something break inside” before they can come to Christ
  • How to comfort believers tormented by fear of the unpardonable sin
  • Why waiting to be “good enough,” or to “feel ready,” keeps many outside the church and away from obedience
  • How to think wisely about miracles, providence, and spiritual existence in a skeptical age
  • Why enjoying God’s service and prayer is not a spiritual danger, and when lack of enjoyment may simply be physical
Whether you are wrestling with doubts yourself or walking alongside someone who is, this classic 1907 work offers warm, practical help. Trumbull refuses shallow answers. Instead, he patiently leads doubting, sensitive souls away from themselves and back to the living Christ—where lasting rest is found. Ideal for pastors, counselors, small group leaders, and any Christian who wants to deal gently and wisely with wounded consciences.

title: The Secret of Divine Contentment
excerpt: “I have learned, in whatever state I am, to be content.” With that single line from Philippians 4:11, Paul introduces a rare grace that...
description: “I have learned, in whatever state I am, to be content.” With that single line from Philippians 4:11, Paul introduces the biblical doctrine of divine contentment—a rare grace that every Christian longs for but few truly enjoy. In this classic work, Puritan pastor Thomas Watson shows that divine contentment is not a natural temperament or the denial of hardship, but a learned, Spirit-taught submission to God’s wise providence. Through clear biblical teaching and warm, practical counsel, Watson exposes the roots of our restlessness and guides believers to see God’s hand in every condition—poverty and plenty, sickness and health, calm and trouble—until the soul can sincerely say, in any circumstance, “I have learned to be content.”

title: Was I Right? A Young Woman’s Choice Between Love and Loyalty to Christ
excerpt: Set in Victorian England and reaching as far as Jerusalem, Was I Right? follows a young woman at the point in life where choices stop being dreams and start becoming consequences.
description:
May is nineteen, and in a moment her whole life changes. Her guardian dies. Her little sister is taken in elsewhere. May herself is left with almost nothing—no income, no home, and no clear future.

Then two very different doors open. One path seems safe and respectable: quiet work in another household, steady and hidden.

The other path promises warmth, protection, and love—but also raises questions she cannot ignore.

Set in Victorian England and reaching as far as Jerusalem, Was I Right? follows a young woman at the point in life where choices stop being dreams and start becoming consequences. It is a story of love, conscience, and what it costs to stay true to Christ when the heart is pulled in two directions.

title: The Art of Enjoying the Bible
excerpt: “How can I read the Bible in a way that I enjoy it?” Edward Leigh Pell takes up that question as plainly...
description: “How can I read the Bible in a way that I enjoy it?” Edward Leigh Pell takes up that question as plainly as a man takes up a lantern in a dark place—not to display the lamp, but to help you see the road. Many know how to read the Scriptures in some outward sense: from duty, to find support for a belief, to learn a fact, or to quiet the conscience. Yet real profit seldom comes where there is no pleasure in the search; no one draws treasure from a mine with a yawn. With homely illustrations and steady counsel, Pell clears away the common misunderstandings that make the Bible seem lifeless or impractical, and shows how the Book “gives back according to how it is approached.” He urges a sane, wakeful, prayerful manner of reading—one that brings the mind and heart into harmony with the Word, until Scripture opens like a flower in its proper air. If you have handled the Bible as a treasure yet have seldom tasted its sweetness, this book will help you turn the stem—and watch the gears begin to move.

title: Training the Child's Heart: Practical Hints for Christian Parents
excerpt: If you’re looking for practical hints for Christian parents, Henry Clay Trumbull offers a refreshing, Scripture-minded...
description: If you’re looking for practical hints for Christian parents, Henry Clay Trumbull offers a refreshing, Scripture-minded approach to child-training—one that made me rethink areas of parenting I hadn’t considered as deeply before. After republishing two of his other works (one on prayer and one on assurance of salvation), I was eager to bring his parenting counsel back into print, and I found it both searching and helpful. Trumbull presses a question many Christian parents need to face: are we helping our children form their own biblical convictions, or are we unintentionally training them to borrow ours without understanding? He urges parents to guide children to test things by the Scriptures—so that, if they differ from us in some areas, they can explain their reasons from the Bible rather than merely saying, “Dad said…” You’ll find chapters that spark thoughtful, practical application—such as Honoring a Child’s Individuality and Training a Child as a Questioner. And while you may not agree with every emphasis (as with any parenting book), Trumbull models a careful pursuit of balance. For example, Will-Training, Rather Than Will-Breaking may strike some readers as too gentle—yet it’s followed by The Place of “Must” in Training, showing he is not avoiding firmness, but aiming for wisdom. In an age of parenting advice that often drifts from Scripture, these practical hints for Christian parents offer biblical perspective and usable counsel—especially on issues you may not have thought through before. What precious souls the Lord has entrusted to us in our children. May God give us grace to love them well.

title: A Bible-Believing Church Must Be Evangelistic
excerpt: Can a church call itself evangelical if it is not evangelistic? In these searching addresses, G. Campbell Morgan brings the whole subject of evangelism...
description: Can a church call itself evangelical if it is not evangelistic? In these searching addresses, G. Campbell Morgan brings the whole subject of evangelism back to its New Testament center: the one unchanging evangel and the living Christ who still proclaims it through His Church. Refusing both nostalgic longing for “old-fashioned revivals” and shallow schemes for a “new evangelism” with no Cross, Morgan insists that there is only one Gospel—always fresh, always sufficient—for every age. Beginning with the evangel itself—Christ’s Lordship, His atoning Cross, His risen life, and His indwelling Spirit—Morgan traces how this message creates and sustains an evangelistic Church. A truly evangelical church, he argues, must be a fellowship of reconciled, regenerate, and reigning believers whose life together makes the Gospel visible to the world.

title: The Reformed Pastor: A Call to Faithful Shepherding
excerpt: This edition of The Reformed Pastor is based on the version described in William Brown’s 1829 Preface—a shortened, carefully edited edition...
description: This edition of The Reformed Pastor is based on the version described in William Brown’s 1829 Preface—a shortened, carefully edited edition (not Palmer’s 1766 abridgment) that removes material unrelated to the main subject and some dated controversy, with minor reordering (including moving “Motives to the Oversight of the Flock” into the main body) and light language correction without modernizing the style. Richard Baxter wrote The Reformed Pastor as a wake-up call to ministers—and a mercy to the church. With plain speech and urgent love, he presses one main point: those who shepherd Christ’s flock must first watch over their own souls, and then labor wisely and personally for the souls entrusted to them. Baxter exposes the dangers that quietly ruin ministry: negligence, pride, fear of man, shallow preaching, and a life that contradicts the message. He calls pastors to repentance, prayerful seriousness, and patient, face-to-face care—catechizing, warning, comforting, and pleading with men as those who must give account. This classic is not theory. It is a pastor speaking to pastors, pleading that the work would not be spoiled by unholy hands. Whether you are a minister, a church leader, or a Christian who wants to understand faithful shepherding, The Reformed Pastor will search you, steady you, and stir you to seek God’s help for work that cannot be done in human strength.

title: The Mother at Home
excerpt: In The Mother at Home audiobook, John Abbott speaks with clear, practical warmth to the daily work of motherhood...
description: In The Mother at Home audiobook, John Abbott speaks with clear, practical warmth to the daily work of motherhood—showing how a mother’s quiet influence shapes a child’s character, habits, and eternal outlook. With vivid examples drawn from ordinary family life, Abbott explains why loving authority matters, how to establish willing obedience without constant conflict, and how a mother can correct faults fairly, wisely, and consistently. This classic Christian guide moves from responsibility to real-world methods: forming order in the home, planning routines that reduce turmoil, addressing a child’s moods with discernment, and giving religious instruction that reaches the heart—not just the memory. The aim is not merely well-behaved children, but a household marked by peace, conscience, reverence, and lasting joy. Written as a companion to The Child at Home, this volume invites mothers (and families) to read together—especially in quiet evening moments—so that counsel becomes conversation, and instruction becomes a shared path toward a more God-honoring home.

title: The Child at Home
excerpt: A companion to The Mother at Home, this classic by John S. C. Abbott speaks to children and teens with a loving but serious aim...
description: A companion to The Mother at Home, this classic by John S. C. Abbott speaks to children and teens with a loving but serious aim: to form hearts that are thankful, truthful, obedient, and ready to live for God, using vivid, true-to-life stories to show how character is shaped one decision at a time. Intended to be read with a parent nearby, it is written not for mere entertainment but for lasting formation, teaching that the path of duty is the path of peace, and that true happiness is found in obedience to Christ.

title: The Six Great Problems of Life: God's Answers
excerpt: What is man—small beneath the vast heavens, yet so precious that God is “mindful of him” and comes near to him? In these chapters, the psalmist’s question (Psalm 8:3–4)
description: What is man—small beneath the vast heavens, yet so precious that God is “mindful of him” and comes near to him? In these chapters, the psalmist’s question (Psalm 8:3–4) becomes a searching journey into God’s original design for humanity and the tragedy of our fallen condition. You will be led to see man as Scripture describes him—body, soul, and spirit—and to understand why the highest life is not found in comfort, culture, or circumstance, but in the spirit made alive to God.

title: Pleasure and Profit in Bible Study
excerpt: D. L. Moody calls believers back to the living power of Scripture. In Pleasure and Profit in Bible Study...
description: Long before Bible study apps and reading plans, D. L. Moody calls believers back to the living power of Scripture. In Pleasure and Profit in Bible Study, this classic work of Moody on Bible Study shows that lasting spiritual growth comes through close, prayerful contact with God’s Word and a deliberate, purposeful approach to reading it. With plainspoken stories and pastoral urgency, Moody warns against both “all work and no Word” and “all Word and no work,” demonstrating how faithful study strengthens communion with God, steadies the Christian life, and equips believers for earnest service. Practical to the core, this timeless presentation of Moody on Bible Study lays out a clear method: ask better questions of every passage, persevere through difficulty, memorize Scripture, and learn to “feed yourself” in the Word—so Bible reading becomes more than a duty and grows into a life-shaping habit.

title: Greed: The Church’s Quiet Sin
excerpt: In Greed: The Church’s Quiet Sin, John Harris exposes the subtle ways covetousness reshapes a congregation—how it dulls prayer...
description: Originally published in 1836. This edition has been revised for readability. Greed rarely announces itself. It settles quietly into the habits of church life—into what we excuse, what we overlook, and what we learn to call “normal.” It can hide behind respectability, careful budgeting, and even religious busyness, while love grows cold and generosity becomes optional. In Greed: The Church’s Quiet Sin, John Harris exposes the subtle ways covetousness reshapes a congregation—how it dulls prayer, weakens compassion, distorts priorities, and slowly turns God’s people inward. With sober clarity and pastoral urgency, he brings Scripture to bear on a sin that many believers fear to name, yet feel daily. This is not a book for pointing fingers at the world. It is a call to the household of faith: to repentance without despair, to stewardship without legalism, and to a renewed joy in laying up treasure in heaven.

title: Grace Abounding: The Conversion of John Bunyan
excerpt: First published in 1666 during John Bunyan’s imprisonment, Grace Abounding to the Chief of Sinners is his spiritual autobiography...
description: First published in 1666 during John Bunyan’s imprisonment, Grace Abounding to the Chief of Sinners is his spiritual autobiography—a record of the Lord’s dealings with his soul in conviction, temptation, conversion, and assurance. Written out of a father’s deep love for his own children, this book is Bunyan’s honest account of God’s work in his heart. Separated from his family by hardship, he records his journey through deep conviction of sin, fearful doubts, fierce temptations, and, at last, the comfort and assurance found in Christ. Bunyan speaks plainly of sinking into despair and being raised again by mercy, showing that God wounds and heals, humbles and restores. What once seemed overwhelming became a testimony to grace greater than his sin and stronger than Satan’s assaults. More than a personal story, this book calls his children—and all who read—to remember the beginnings of God’s grace in their own lives and to press on through the wilderness until they reach the promised rest beyond.

title: Have Mercy Upon Me
excerpt: In Have Mercy Upon Me, Andrew Murray leads the reader through Psalm 51 with warmth, clarity, and spiritual seriousness, showing what true repentance really is...
description: In Have Mercy Upon Me, Andrew Murray leads the reader through Psalm 51 with warmth, clarity, and spiritual seriousness, showing what true repentance really is and how a sinner finds mercy with God. This is not a vague treatment of grace, but a deeply searching and practical call to honest confession, personal cleansing, and living faith in the mercy God gives through Christ. Again and again, Murray presses the reader beyond mere religious words and toward a real experience of forgiveness, renewal, and restored fellowship with God. Rich with pastoral counsel and gospel hope, this devotional work is especially suited for burdened consciences, seeking hearts, and any believer who wants to better understand the depth of sin, the necessity of repentance, and the fullness of divine mercy.

title: The Young Husband: A Man’s Duties in Marriage
excerpt: Marriage, in William Alcott’s vision, is not merely a private arrangement or a pursuit of personal happiness. It is a calling...
description: Marriage, in William Alcott’s vision, is not merely a private arrangement or a pursuit of personal happiness. It is a calling. In The Young Husband, Alcott urges the young husband to think seriously about his responsibilities before God, toward his wife, and within the home he is building. With unusual directness, The Young Husband addresses the habits that shape family life: choosing an occupation wisely, establishing order in the home, leading in family worship, guarding conversation, controlling temper, honoring one’s wife, using time well, caring for health, and showing kindness in daily life. Again and again, Alcott returns to one central conviction: the character and conduct of a young husband leave a deep mark on the happiness and future of his household. Far from being abstract or theoretical, The Young Husband is filled with practical counsel for everyday life. It speaks to the young husband who wants his home to be governed not by selfishness, carelessness, or harshness, but by thoughtfulness, discipline, tenderness, and Christian principle. This revised edition of The Young Husband presents Alcott’s counsel in clearer language while preserving the seriousness, moral force, and practical wisdom of the original work. A challenging and deeply useful Christian classic on marriage, home life, and the calling of a young husband to lead his household well.

title: The Missionary Who Would Not Turn Back: François Coillard on the Zambezi
excerpt: Discover the remarkable life of François Coillard, the missionary pioneer known as the Pathfinder on the Upper Zambezi. Drawn from Edward Shillito’s Francois Coillard, A Wayfaring Man...
description: Discover the remarkable life of François Coillard, the missionary pioneer known as the Pathfinder on the Upper Zambezi. Drawn from Edward Shillito’s Francois Coillard, A Wayfaring Man, this biography traces Coillard’s journey from a humble village in France to the hardships of missionary labor in Basutoland and Barotseland. Alongside his brave wife Christina, he endured loneliness, rejection, sickness, political danger, and deep personal loss, yet remained steadfast in preaching Christ. This account highlights Coillard’s years of labor at Leribe, his bold witness before African chiefs, his work in translation and hymn writing, and his perseverance through some of the most trying conditions a missionary could face. It is a moving story of sacrifice, courage, sorrow, and unwavering devotion to the Gospel. Beneath a great tree at Sefula, where François and Christina were laid to rest, the words on their memorial fittingly sum up his life: To live is Christ.

title: The Weapon of Prayer
excerpt: In The Weapon of Prayer, E. M. Bounds argues that prayer is not mere sentiment, routine, or religious formality. It is the God-ordained means through which God blesses His people, strengthens His church, empowers preaching, and advances the Gospel.
description:
What if prayer is not a secondary part of the Christian life, but one of God’s chief appointed means for advancing His work in the world? In The Weapon of Prayer, E. M. Bounds argues that prayer is not mere sentiment, routine, or religious formality. It is the God-ordained means through which God blesses His people, strengthens His church, empowers preaching, and advances the Gospel. With urgency and conviction, Bounds confronts prayerlessness in ordinary believers, in church life, and in the pulpit. He calls Christians back to earnest, persevering, God-dependent prayer. Drawing from Scripture and from the lives of devoted men and women of prayer, this book shows that spiritual power, holy living, effective ministry, and true revival are inseparably tied to prayer. Inside this book, you will find:
  • why prayer is essential to God’s work
  • how prayerlessness weakens believers and churches
  • the need for praying pastors, leaders, and Christians
  • the connection between prayer, preaching, and spiritual power
  • historical examples of lives marked by earnest prayer
This 2026 edition has been revised for readability while preserving the force of the original 1931 work. If you want a deeper prayer life, stronger dependence on God, and renewed seriousness about His kingdom, this classic Christian work on prayer will challenge and encourage you.

title: The Path of Prayer
excerpt: If prayer has ever felt distant, difficult, or mysterious, The Path of Prayer will draw you back to its true heart. Samuel Chadwick writes...
description: If prayer has ever felt distant, difficult, or mysterious, The Path of Prayer will draw you back to its true heart. Samuel Chadwick writes with the kind of spiritual weight that comes not from theory, but from a lifetime of seeking God. He leads the reader into prayer not as a religious exercise, but as a living fellowship with the Father—learned in secret, nourished by Scripture, shaped by the Spirit, and deepened through real communion with God. Chadwick was not only a powerful teacher on prayer in his own right, but also a formative influence on Leonard Ravenhill, who studied at Cliff College during Chadwick’s years there and later became known worldwide for his own burden for prayer and revival. That connection gives this book added significance: here you are hearing one of the voices that helped shape a later generation of revival preaching. Warm, searching, and deeply practical, this book speaks to weary believers, hungry seekers, and Christians who long for more than shallow devotion. It deals honestly with persistence, faith, discipline, spiritual power, and even the problem of unanswered prayer. The Path of Prayer does not merely talk about prayer—it urges you into the presence of God and makes you want to begin.

title: The Unnoticed Sins of Everyday Speech
excerpt: In The Unnoticed Sins of Everyday Speech, Amelia Opie exposes the quiet, respectable ways people wound one another with their words.
description: In The Unnoticed Sins of Everyday Speech, Amelia Opie exposes the quiet, respectable ways people wound one another with their words. Moving beyond obvious slander, she traces everyday gossip, criticism, mockery, and careless conversation back to deeper roots—pride, rivalry, vanity, envy, and an unchecked desire to be noticed. Through vivid examples drawn from family life, society, religion, and friendship, she shows how easily the tongue becomes an instrument of harm while the heart remains blind to its own motives. This is a searching and often convicting work on the hidden sins that thrive in ordinary conversation. Opie does not merely condemn harmful speech; she reveals the self-love and competition that often give birth to it, and she calls readers to greater humility, self-examination, and charity. Though written in the nineteenth century, the book remains strikingly relevant in a world still filled with rumor, comparison, and careless words. Read with discernment, The Unnoticed Sins of Everyday Speech offers a sobering mirror to the heart and a serious plea to govern the tongue with wisdom, restraint, and Christian love.

title: In Quest of God
description: Heard of Hudson Taylor and Pastor Hsi? In this biography, Marshall Broomhall brings to light another remarkable account from the spread of the gospel in China—the story of Pastor Chang and Ch’ü Wan-yih. One a Buddhist priest, the other a respected scholar, both were sincere seekers who found themselves dissatisfied with the answers their traditions could give. Their journey begins quietly, with a forgotten Gospel discovered in a temple. With no teacher to guide them, they begin reading the Scriptures for themselves. What follows is a steady unfolding—questions, conviction, growing understanding, and a turning away from old beliefs. As the truth takes hold, their lives begin to change in ways that bring real cost: opposition from authorities, loss of honor, personal trials, and suffering. Yet through it all, their faith deepens, and their testimony spreads. Set against the backdrop of rural China, famine, and the early days of inland mission work, In Quest of God traces how God draws men to Himself and uses them to reach others. From temple life to pastoral ministry, from isolation to fellowship with other believers, this is a story of transformation that is both simple and profound. Clear, engaging, and rooted in true events, this book offers a glimpse into the quiet but powerful work of the gospel in hard places. It is a reminder that God still meets those who seek Him and that even the smallest beginnings can lead to lasting fruit.
